request.question: KOIL ran a radio show featuring Mark Levin who worked for which Attorney General?

title: George J. Terwilliger III
Mark Levin
WHLO
The Liberty Amendments
WNIX
Earth 2 (TV series)
KOIL
WWLZ
WTMA
The Mark Levin Show
sentences: George J. Terwilliger III (born June 5, 1950) is an American lawyer and politician.
He is a partner in the Washington, D.C. office of McGuireWoods LLP and is a former United States Deputy Attorney General and acting United States Attorney General.
Terwilliger, of Vermont, was nominated on February 14, 1992, by President George H.W. Bush to be Deputy Attorney General at the U.S. Department of Justice.
He would succeed William Pelham Barr.

As Deputy Attorney General, Terwilliger became the second-highest-ranking official in the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) and ran the day-to-day operations of the Department, serving in that position from 1991 through 1993.
He was appointed to the position after serving as the United States Attorney for the District of Vermont.
In addition, he currently serves on the Advisory Board of Intellaine, LLP, a U.S. defense and risk engineering firm located in Arlington, Virginia.
Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.
He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".
Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.
He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".
On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.
WHLO (640 AM) – branded 640 WHLO – is a commercial radio station licensed to Akron, Ohio, serving both the Akron and Canton metro areas.
Owned by iHeartMedia, the station broadcasts primarily a news/talk format.

WHLO is the local affiliate for Fox News Radio; conservative talk programs "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show", "The Mark Levin Show", and "The Schnitt Show"; and overnight night talk shows "The Dave Ramsey Show" and "Coast to Coast AM".
The station also serves as the radio home of the Kent State Golden Flashes.
The WHLO studios are located in North Canton, while the station transmitter resides in Copley.
In addition to a standard analog transmission, WHLO is available online via iHeartRadio.
The Liberty Amendments: Restoring the American Republic is a book by the American talk radio host and lawyer Mark Levin, published in 2013.
In it, Levin lays out and makes a case for eleven Constitutional amendments which he believes would restore the Constitution’s chief components: federalism, republicanism, and limited government.
WNIX (1330 AM) is a radio station broadcasting a News-Talk format, featuring Rush Limbaugh, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, Doug Stephan and Dave Ramsey.
Licensed to Greenville, Mississippi, USA, the station is currently owned by Delta Radio Network LLC.
Earth 2 is an American science fiction television series which aired on NBC from November 6, 1994 to June 4, 1995.
The show was canceled after one season of 22 episodes.

It follows the journey and settlement of a small expeditionary group called the Eden Project, with the intent to journey to an Earth-like planet called G889 in an attempt to find a cure to an illness called "the syndrome".

The series was created by Michael Duggan, Carol Flint, Mark Levin, and Billy Ray, produced by Amblin Entertainment and Universal Television, and filmed primarily in northern New Mexico around the Santa Fe area.
The series' music was composed by David Bergeaud, and the executive producers were Michael Duggan, Mark Levin, and Carol Flint.
KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.
It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.
KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.
It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.
KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.
KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.
WWLZ (820 AM) is a radio station in the Elmira-Corning market in New York state.

It is a talk radio station that airs the Premiere Networks lineup ("Glenn Beck Radio Program", "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show" and "Coast to Coast AM") during the workday and overnight, Cumulus Media Networks programs ("First Light", "Imus in the Morning", "The Mark Levin Show", "The John Batchelor Show") during the evening and in morning drive, MRN races and Elmira Jackals hockey.
WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.
This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.
WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.
The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.
They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.
Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.
Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.
The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".

The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

hop1_docs: Mark Levin | Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.  He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".  Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

KOIL | KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.  It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.  KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.  It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.  KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.  KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.

The Mark Levin Show | The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.  The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".  The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

Mark Levine (journalist) | Mark H. Levine (born May 7, 1966) is the Democratic Delegate to the Virginia House of Delegates from Virginia's 45th District, which encompasses the eastern half of the City of Alexandria, some of the northern West End, parts of South Arlington, and the Alexandria portion of Fairfax County.  A Constitutional lawyer, Levine served as Legislative Counsel to Congressman Barney Frank (D-MA) from 2001 to 2003 and was an early advocate in the "Marriage Equality” movement.  Since 2003, Levine has hosted a nationally syndicated progressive public policy radio program and worked as a TV pundit.  In 2014, Levine ran in the Democratic Primary for Virginia's 8th Congressional District last year, a seat currently held by Rep. Don Beyer.

WCHM | WCHM (1490 AM) and 96.7 FM is a radio station broadcasting a News Talk Information format.  Licensed to Clarkesville, Georgia, USA.  The station is currently owned by WCHM Radio, LLC, an affiliated company of Batten Communications, inc. and features programming from Premiere Radio Networks and FOX News Radio.  Major shows include Rush Limbaugh, Glenn Beck, Mark Levin, Sean Hannity, Clark Howard, Michael Savage and Coast to Coast with George Noory.  The station also features local programming and University of Georgia sports programming.

Ketzel Levine | Ketzel Levine is an American radio journalist who began her broadcast career in 1974.  She joined National Public Radio (NPR) in 1977 and worked, variously, as the network's arts producer, sports director, features reporter and garden expert.  From 2000 through 2008, she was senior correspondent for the NPR program "Morning Edition".  At the end of that year, due to cutbacks at the network, Levine was laid off, while working on a documentary series about Americans coping with economic stress and job loss.  Her final NPR broadcast was about how she, herself, had just lost her job.

WTMA | WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.  This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.  WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.  The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.  They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.  Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.  Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - KOIL page (passage 2) lists syndicated hosts including "Mark Levin" (quote: "Mark Levin") — KOIL.
  - Mark Levin bio (passage 1) states he "was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese" (quote: "chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese") — Mark Levin.
- Ambiguities:
  - Similar name present: "Mark Levine" (passage 4) is a different person and appears as a candidate; do not assume which is intended.
  - KOIL passage names "Mark Levin" but does not itself show the Levin bio details that link to Edwin Meese.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as in passages): "Mark Levin" and "Edwin Meese".
  - Missing fact to confirm: that the "Mark Levin" listed on KOIL is the same Mark Levin (the lawyer/radio host) who "was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese."
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Mark Levin" page — check lede/infobox and "Career" or "Early life and career" sections for the Edwin Meese chief-of-staff statement and identifying info (birth year, occupations).
     - Search keywords: "Mark Levin Edwin Meese chief of staff lede infobox".
  2) Open the "KOIL" page — check the station schedule/weekday lineup section to confirm the listed "Mark Levin" show and any linked page or context that identifies the host.
     - Search keywords: "KOIL Mark Levin schedule weekday shows".
  3) Open "Edwin Meese" page — check lede/infobox and "Career" or "Justice Department" sections to confirm his Attorney General role and any mention of Chief of Staff Mark Levin.
     - Search keywords: "Edwin Meese Attorney General chief of staff Mark Levin".
  4) (If needed) Open "The Mark Levin Show" page — check lede/infobox for syndicated affiliates and host identification.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and identity linkage is confirmed.
response.hop2_query: Mark Levin chief of staff Edwin Meese
hop2_docs: Mark Levin | Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.  He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".  Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

Meese Report | The final report of the Attorney General's Commission on Pornography (usually referred to as (the) Meese Report, for U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese) is the result of a comprehensive investigation into pornography ordered by U.S. President Ronald Reagan.  It was published in July 1986 and contains 1,960 pages.

Edwin Meese | Edwin Meese III (born December 2, 1931) is an American attorney, law professor, author and member of the Republican Party who served in official capacities within the Ronald Reagan Gubernatorial Administration (1967–1974), the Reagan Presidential Transition Team (1980) and the Reagan White House (1981–1985), eventually rising to hold the position of the 75th Attorney General of the United States (1985–1988).

Arnold Burns | Arnold Irwin Burns (April 14, 1930 – October 1, 2013) was an American lawyer.  He served as the United States Deputy Attorney General from 1986 to 1988 under President Ronald Reagan and U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese.  In March 1988, Burns, together with the head of the U.S. Justice Department's criminal division William Weld and four aides, resigned from office in protest of what they viewed as improper conduct by Attorney General Meese, including personal financial indiscretions.  In July 1988, Burns and Weld jointly testified before the U.S. Congress in support of a potential prosecution of Meese following an investigation by a special prosecutor, who had declined to file charges.  Meese resigned from office later in July 1988, shortly after Burns and Weld appeared before Congress.

Edwin Bramall | Field Marshal Edwin Noel Westby Bramall, Baron Bramall, {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (born 18 December 1923) is a retired senior British Army officer.  He served as Chief of the General Staff, the professional head of the British Army, between 1979 and 1982, and as Chief of the Defence Staff, professional head of the British Armed Forces, from 1982 to 1985.  He developed the concept of the "Fifth Pillar" pulling together the activities of defence attachés to form a structure for intervention in smaller countries.

Blair Levin | Blair Steven Levin is an American lawyer formerly with the Federal Communications Commission, who served as the Executive Director of the National Broadband Plan from 2009 to 2010.  During the Presidency of Bill Clinton he was chief of staff to FCC chairman Reed Hundt from 1993 to 1997.

Maryland Heights, Missouri | Maryland Heights is a second-ring west-central suburb of St. Louis, located in St. Louis County, Missouri, United States.  The population was 27,472 at the 2010 census.  The city was incorporated in 1985.  Edwin L. Dirck was elected the city's first mayor.  Mark M. Levin served as City Administrator from August 1985 to 2015.  Mr. Levin is now an associate professor at Indiana University Bloomington.
response.summary_2: "was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese". Mark Levin is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality, born September 21, 1957. Edwin Meese III is an American attorney who served as the 75th Attorney General of the United States (1985–1988).
